coredata.dc:title: A novel hybrid equivalent circuit model for lithium-ion battery considering nonlinear capacity effects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2021 6th International Conference on Advances on Clean Energy Research
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Dongdong
Xiao, Long
Yan, Wenduan
Guo, Yinbiao
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel hybrid equivalent circuit model for lithium-ion battery. The proposed equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion battery is based on Thevenin equivalent circuit model, and a state-of-charge (SOC) part is added into the model to improve the model performance. In the proposed model, the battery capacity is divided into available capacity and unavailable capacity, which can well reflect the rated capacity effect when the battery is working, the recovery effect when the battery is not working and the self-discharge effect at all times. At the same time, a new definition of the SOC is given. The SOC value of the battery is estimated in real time through the model of the charged state part, and the one-to-one correspondence between SOC and open circuit voltage is established. Besides, the second-order polarized RC network in Thevenin model is improved to the third-order RC network, and the third-order RC network is endowed with practical physical significance, to reflect the polarization effect of the battery more accurately. Finally, Simulink simulation and experimental results have verified the correctness of the proposed model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State of charge (SOC)
Rated capacity effect
Recovery effect
Equivalent circuit model
Available/ unavailable capacity

O C V

, R2

Novel SOC definition considering unavailable capacity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hybrid equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion battery with nonlinear capacity effects.
The curve of open circuit voltage VOCV changing with DOD.
The analysis to the state-of-charge part of the proposed model.
The curve of the hybrid power pulse test.
The discharge curve of lithium ion battery in various discharge rates.
Change of available capacity and unavailable capacity in the battery model.
Comparison of SOC estimation with different definition.
Linearization of the function VOCV(DOD) using segmentation of the curve.
Fundamental characteristics of lithium ion battery ICR18650-22P.
Experimental data summarize of the battery under different discharge ratio.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 6th International Conference on Advances on Clean Energy Research, ICACER 2021 April 15–17,​ 2021, Barcelona, Spain

head.ce:title: A novel hybrid equivalent circuit model for lithium-ion battery considering nonlinear capacity effects

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic and Electrical Engineering, Minnan University of Science and Technology, Quanzhou 362700, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Electrical Engineering, Minnan University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Electronic and Electrical Engineering, Minnan University of Science and Technology, Quanzhou 362700, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Electrical Engineering, Minnan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel hybrid equivalent circuit model for lithium-ion battery. The proposed equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion battery is based on Thevenin equivalent circuit model, and a state-of-charge (SOC) part is added into the model to improve the model performance. In the proposed model, the battery capacity is divided into available capacity and unavailable capacity, which can well reflect the rated capacity effect when the battery is working, the recovery effect when the battery is not working and the self-discharge effect at all times. At the same time, a new definition of the SOC is given. The SOC value of the battery is estimated in real time through the model of the charged state part, and the one-to-one correspondence between SOC and open circuit voltage is established. Besides, the second-order polarized RC network in Thevenin model is improved to the third-order RC network, and the third-order RC network is endowed with practical physical significance, to reflect the polarization effect of the battery more accurately. Finally, Simulink simulation and experimental results have verified the correctness of the proposed model.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy storage technology is widely used in power systems, electric vehicles and other fields, and has become a research focus today. Among many energy storage carriers, lithium-ion batteries have received a lot of attention due to their large specific energy, long cycle life, small self-discharge, no memory effect, and environmental friendliness [1]. To effectively simulate actual lithium-ion batteries, an appropriate battery model is essential. The model is required to reflect the state of charge (SOC) of the battery, and be able to simulate the battery’s output voltage, current curve, and other inherent characteristics of the battery. At the same time, the model should have both low complexity and high accuracy.

Therefore, experts and scholars have done more research on battery modeling. Commonly used battery models include electrochemical model, neural network model and equivalent circuit model. The equivalent circuit model simulates the transient and steady state characteristics of the battery through electrical components. The advantages of this modeling are: (1) simple modeling method, (2) easy parameter identification, (3) high precision, (4) facilitate the integration of multiple factors, (5) convenient for mathematical analysis, (6) it can simulate the full range of battery SOC, (7) strong applicability, and so on.

Typical equivalent circuit models of lithium-ion batteries include Rint model, Thevenin model and PNGV model [2]. Equivalent circuit models usually have second-order or higher-order RC networks that represent battery polarization effects, such as Thevenin model. Through the identification of specific parameters R and C, an accurate model can be established [3]. In a compromise between model complexity and accuracy, a second-order or third-order RC network is more appropriate as the polarization internal resistance of the battery. The model is divided into a state of charge part and a V-I characteristic part, which can simulate the transient and steady-state characteristics of the battery while reflecting the changes of the battery’s capacity, SOC, and running time [4]. As a bridge between the state of charge and the V-I characteristics, there is a non-linear corresponding relationship between the SOC of a general battery and its open circuit voltage, the Shepherd model can be used to fit [5], and the curve of open circuit voltage and SOC is fitted piecewise linearly with the least square method [6].

The KiBaM model is an intuitive battery model that can describe the non-linear capacity effect of the battery, thereby helping to estimate the battery SOC and predict the running time. However, it cannot describe the dynamic V-I characteristics of the battery, and it cannot be combined with other circuits or systems to carry out a joint simulation [7]. The Puckett equation can make up for the deficiencies of the KiBaM model and help quantitatively describe the non-linear capacity effect of the battery [8]. However, the equation is not very accurate in fitting the curve of the lithium-ion battery.

Aiming at the non-linear capacity effect of the battery (including the rated capacity effect and the recovery effect), this paper proposes a hybrid equivalent circuit model including the state of charge part and the V-I characteristic part in Section 2, which can more accurately reflects the conversion of available/unavailable battery capacity and the corresponding SOC changes when the battery discharges at high current. The Section 3 analyzes the model in detail, determines the model parameters and defines the battery SOC [9]. The Section 4 illustrates the effectiveness of all aspects of the model through simulation and experimental analysis. Finally, Section 4 concludes this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed novel model of lithium-ion battery
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the accuracy of reflecting the various states of the battery and optimize the management of the battery, experts and scholars have proposed various types of battery models. Among them, the equivalent circuit model is favored because of its obvious advantages. This paper proposes a hybrid equivalent circuit battery model that can mainly reflect the nonlinear capacity effect of lithium batteries.
The lithium-ion battery model proposed in this article is divided into two parts, namely the state of charge part and the V-I characteristic part, as shown in Fig. 1.

Generally, lithium-ion batteries are charged, and the charging current is strictly limited. For example, the maximum continuous charging current of ICR18650-22P is 1C, which is 2150 mA. The discharge current is relatively limited, as long as it is less than the maximum current of 10 A. Therefore, the focus of research on battery charging and discharging at large rates can be focused on the discharge, so
i
cell
is considered positive in the proposed model. In addition, the various characteristics of the battery are greatly affected by temperature. This article does not consider the effect of temperature on the battery for the time being, and the experiments are all completed at room temperature 25 °C.
The Proposed model can describe the following characteristics of lithium-ion batteries.

(1) V-I characteristics: Simulate the output characteristics of the battery in steady state similar to the constant voltage source, the polarization effect in the transient state, and the gradual change of the open circuit voltage with the SOC.
(2) Self-discharge: An irreversible reaction occurs inside the battery, causing the loss of battery capacity. Use parallel resistance to simulate.
(3) Polarization effect: When the battery has current, its internal electrodes deviate from the equilibrium electrode potential. Use RC network to simulate.

(4) Rated capacity effect and recovery effect: the greater the discharge rate of the battery, the less power it can release. However, regardless of battery heat loss, the part of the electricity that is not released due to the large discharge rate, that is, the unusable capacity, is still stored in the battery. After the battery is left to stand or discharged with a small current for a period of time, the phenomenon that unusable capacity will be converted into usable capacity is the recovery effect. The state of charge part of the model can simulate the rated capacity effect and the recovery effect.

The battery used in this analysis is Samsung’s ICR18650-22P lithium-ion battery. This paper establishes an equivalent circuit model for this battery and determines the values of related parameters in the model. The focus is on the state of charge part of the battery model.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Open circuit voltage V
O C V
ce:section.ce:para: For lithium-ion batteries, the open circuit voltage has a strong correspondence with the battery’s SOC. However, in practice, it is not convenient to use the terminal voltage method or the open circuit voltage method to estimate the SOC of the battery. The reason is: due to the different charge–discharge modes and charge–discharge rates, under the same SOC, the terminal voltage of a lithium-ion battery will be significantly different, and it is not convenient to use the terminal voltage to reflect its open circuit voltage; the acquisition of the open circuit voltage requires the battery to stand still or Discharge with a small current for a long enough time is time-consuming and inefficient.

In a normal working environment, the open circuit voltage V
O C V
of the battery has a stable relationship with the SOC, and it is not affected by temperature. It can be considered that the open circuit voltage V
O C V
is solely determined by the SOC. Through experiments, the discharge curve of the lithium-ion battery ICR18650-22P at a discharge rate of 0.1C is obtained. As shown in Fig. 2, it can be approximated that the curve is the variation of the open circuit voltage with DOD (depth of discharge, DOD
= 1-SOC) curve.

In order to express the functional relationship between the open circuit voltage V
O C V
and DOD concisely, the curve in Fig. 2. can be divided into n segments of approximate straight lines, and each segment is fitted with least squares regression and expressed linearly, as in Eq. (1), where i
∈ [1,n].
(1)
V
O C V
= f
( D O D )
=
a
i
⋅ D O D +
b
i

The parameters of each linear regression are listed in Table 1, and the fitting effect is good through R 2 test. Eq. (2) is the piecewise function of V
O C V
concerning DOD.
(2)
V
O C V
( D O D )
=
− 1 . 5644 ⋅ D O D + 4 . 1315 ,
D O D ∈
[ 0 . 000 , 0 . 103 )
− 0 . 6094 ⋅ D O D + 4 . 0403 ,
D O D ∈
[ 0 . 103 , 0 . 327 )
− 1 . 1384 ⋅ D O D + 4 . 2066 ,
D O D ∈

− 0 . 4568 ⋅ D O D + 3 . 8233 ,

− 1 . 0374 ⋅ D O D + 4 . 2951 ,
D O D ∈
[ 0 . 827 , 0 . 960 )
− 13 . 767 ⋅ D O D + 16 . 617 ,
D O D ∈
[ 0 . 960 , 1 . 000 ]
This function serves as a bridge connecting the state of charge part on the left side with the V-I characteristic part on the right side of the model as shown in Fig. 1.

In the model proposed in this paper, the part of the state of charge on the left can well describe the nonlinear capacity effect of the battery. Ignoring the self-discharge effect of the battery, the battery capacity is divided into available capacity and unavailable capacity. The capacitor C 1 stores part of the available capacity, the capacitor C 2 stores the unavailable capacity and the other part of the available capacity, as shown in Fig. 3.

Eq. (3) shows the mathematical relationship of several key parameters in the model of Fig. 3. By combining mathematical equation with images, it can help to deepen understanding of the model.
(3)

Since the state of charge part of the designed model is composed of two capacitors and a resistor, the equivalent capacity of the battery can be calculated from the rated capacity and rated voltage of the battery, which is
C =
Q
total
/
U
1
. Now it is necessary to determine the state of charge part of the model. The ratio of capacitance value. The capacitance value ratio is closely related to the discharge rate of the battery: at a large discharge rate, the available capacity stored in
C
1
is greatly reduced; at a small discharge rate, the unusable capacity stored in
C
2
is greatly reduced, and this article is charged The state part model will degenerate into a model in which
C
1
is connected in parallel with the current source, and
R
2
and
C
2
will degenerate and disappear.

The battery discharges its electric energy at a certain discharge rate. At this time, the voltage at the C1 terminal is 0 V. According to this property, the ratio of C 1 to C 2 is calculated. By counting the time required for battery power discharge under different discharge rates, the ratio of each available capacity can be obtained. User to represent the ratio, and r, C 1, and C 2 are functions of the discharge rate I, we can get C 1(I) =
r(I) ⋅
C and C 2(I) = [1 −
r(I)] ⋅
C. The method of obtaining R 2 will be given below to determine the state of charge part of the model.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Resistance of R
d i s c h a r g e
, R2
ce:section.ce:para:
For batteries with better quality, the self-discharge effect is not obvious, and the effect will be obvious only when it is not used for a long time. This article will not go into further details. It is considered that the parallel resistance
R
discharge
is large and can be ignored.

The value of the resistance R 2 relates to the non-linear capacity effect of the battery, which is analyzed in detail in this paper. The rate at which the capacitor C 2 discharges depends on the size of the resistor R 2 and the voltage difference between the two capacitors. When the voltage on the capacitor C 1 is 0 V, it means that the battery is exhausted, that is, the SOC is 0. The change of the electric quantity on the two capacitors is shown in Eq. (4).
(4)
d
Q

When the battery is in a constant current I discharge state, due to the existence of the resistor
R
2
, the voltage on the capacitor
C
1
will change faster than the
C
2
, and the voltage difference between the two capacitors will gradually increase. Therefore, this part of the charge stored in
C
2
caused by the voltage difference of
C
1
and
C
2
is an unavailable capacity. When the discharge current decreases or the battery does not discharge, the charge will enter
C
1
from
C
2
through
R
2
until the voltage difference between the two capacitors is 0 V. Therefore, compared to the high current discharge, the battery will have more charge to be used when the battery is discharged at a low current or in a static state. This explains the non-linear capacity effect of the battery including the rated capacity effect and the recovery effect.

δ (t) is the voltage difference between the two capacitors, which is a very critical parameter when describing the nonlinear capacity effect of the battery. The expression of unavailable capacity u(t) is shown in Eq. (7).

In Eq. (7), the exponential part of the polynomial tends to 0 when the time t is large. It can be seen that the unavailable capacity of the battery will gradually approach a limit value as the discharge time increases, that is, the u

is the potential available capacity that is still stored inside the battery when the battery is discharged. The final unavailable capacity value of the battery under a certain constant discharge current I is obtained through experiments. The two formulas in Eq. (8) can be combined to finally obtain R 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal resistance and each polarization resistance and capacitance
ce:section.ce:para: At the moment when a constant current discharge current is applied to the battery, the battery terminal voltage drops suddenly, because the constant current produces a voltage drop on the ohmic internal resistance of the battery. Divide this voltage drop by the constant current value to obtain the ohmic internal resistance; after the battery voltage drops suddenly, there will be a slow drop process, which is the polarization effect of the battery. The battery terminal voltage change curve corresponding to the polarization effect can be connected in series by a multi-stage RC parallel network, and a model corresponding to the battery polarization effect can be obtained by simulating the low, intermediate, and high frequency bands of the curve. In the same way, the ohmic internal resistance and the polarization resistance and capacitance can also be obtained from the voltage curve of the constant current charging terminal of the battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Novel SOC definition considering unavailable capacity
ce:section.ce:para:
The traditional SOC definition, such as Eq. (9), is commonly used in low-current rate discharge of batteries. However, the SOC under this definition does not consider the existence of unavailable capacity under high-current rate discharge. When using this definition for estimation, the estimation accuracy is very unsatisfactory.
(9)
S O C
( t )
= S O C
(
t
0
)
−
1
Q
total
∫
t
0
t
i
cell
d τ

In fact, when the battery is discharged, an extra part of the potential usable capacity stored in C2 will be converted into unusable capacity, causing the battery’s SOC to drop faster. When estimating the battery SOC, this paper takes the nonlinear capacity effect of the battery into consideration, as shown in Eq. (10).
(10)
S O C
( t )
=
Q
available
Q
total
= S O C
(
t
0
)
−
1
Q
total
[
∫
t

t
i
cell
d τ +
Q
unavailable
( t )
]
S O C
(
t
0
)
−
1
Q
total
[
∫
t
0
t
i
cell
d τ + u
( t )
]
This definition takes the unusable capacity of the battery as part of the battery’s lost power, which can make the estimated SOC value closer to the true value.
The experiment performed constant current discharge on the battery ICR18650-22P. The battery performance parameters are shown in Table 2.
After performing discharge experiments with various currents, the discharge curves of the battery at various discharge rates were obtained, as shown in Fig. 5.

As shown in Fig. 4, this cluster of discharge curves at different rates shows that the battery can release a small amount of electricity at a large discharge rate, and the terminal voltage is lower, which fully reflects the battery’s rated capacity effect. In extreme cases, the battery can only discharge 60% of the rated capacity at 4.65C discharge rate (10 A), and the nonlinear capacity effect is quite obvious.

Use the measured discharge duration, discharge capacity, and other data at the discharge rate of 3C to simulate the state of charge part of the previous battery model to illustrate the battery’s recovery effect and the superiority of the novel defined SOC.
In Table 3, the change in the discharge state of the battery at a discharge rate of 3C is used to illustrate the model.

At a discharge rate of 3C (6.45 A), the battery is discharged at a constant current for 17 min (1020 s), and a total of 85% of the rated capacity is discharged. Under the 3C discharge rate, the unavailable capacity of the battery is
u
max
= 2150 − 1828 = 322 mAh = 1159.2 As from the experiment.

Eq.(8) combined with the unavailable capacity value of the battery 1159.2 As under the discharge rate I = 6.45 A, it can be solved that R 2
= 0.199 Ω . After determining the parameters of the state-of-charge part of the model according to the experimental data, the state-of-charge part can be simulated. The simulation results are shown in Fig. 6.

Fig. 6 reflects the waveform curve of the battery’s internal available capacity, unavailable capacity and released power when the battery is continuously discharged for 1020 s at a high current rate of 3C (6.45 A) until the battery reaches the cut-off voltage of 2.75 V. The available capacity gradually decreases with time until the power is exhausted. After the battery is disconnected, the unavailable capacity stored in
C
2
is gradually converted into available capacity. The unavailable capacity increases at the initial stage of battery discharge and gradually approaches a limit value, and then the unavailable capacity remains stable. After the battery is disconnected, the unavailable capacity is gradually converted into available capacity and decreases to zero.

Regardless of the non-linear capacity effect of the battery, the battery SOC = 0.15 at 1020 s, but the experiment shows that the battery has reached the cut-off voltage at this time, so the corresponding SOC is zero is more appropriate. The traditional definition of SOC is only suitable for small current discharge occasions.

As shown in Fig. 7, the SOC curve under the basic definition is like the black dotted line. A more accurate SOC definition proposed in this paper, is shown in Eq. (10), which can estimate the current battery SOC at all times, and can predict the remaining working time of the battery under constant current discharge. The SOC curve under the novel definition is shown as the solid blue line in Fig. 7. Comparing these two curves and combining the battery’s exhaustion at t = 1020 s, it is found that the novel defined SOC curve is closer to the actual situation.

This paper establishes a hybrid equivalent circuit model for lithium-ion batteries, divides the model into a state of charge part and a V-I characteristic part, and uses the functional relationship between the battery’s open circuit voltage and SOC as a connection bridge. Both parts of the model can reflect the characteristics of all aspects of the lithium-ion battery, which is a more comprehensive equivalent circuit model.

In view of the non-linear capacity effect of the battery, the model divides the battery capacity into two, namely the available capacity and the unusable capacity. It better describes the attenuation of the battery capacity under high current rate discharge, and the capacity after standing still. There has been a recovery. The model was deduced in detail, and the specific parameters of each element in the state of charge of the model were determined to meet the requirements of simulation under different discharge rates. Combining simulation and experimental data shows the effectiveness of the model.

Meanwhile, when defining the SOC, this article considers the unusable capacity of the battery, which can improve the accuracy of battery SOC estimation. The basic definition of SOC is compared with the SOC defined in this article, combined with the actual state of charge of the battery and the comparison of the SOC estimation curves under the two definitions, to illustrate that the SOC defined in this article has advantages in estimating batteries under high current discharge. Under the low current discharge rate, R2 and C2 in the charged state part of the model will degenerate and disappear. Only the capacitor C1 is connected in parallel with the current source i
cell
. At this time, the unusable battery capacity is always 0, and the Eqs. (9) and (10) change is equal.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province, China under Grant 2019KF2307, Science and Technology Project of Quanzhou City, China under Grant 2020C011R and 2019CT003, and Fujian Province Young and Middle-aged Teacher Education Research Project, China under Grant JAT200739.
